reasoning_model_1: The model successfully reads the text with high accuracy. Regarding the Non-Linguistic Vocalization (smack of the lips), the model interprets the text 'Mm' as a prolonged, resonant 'Hmm' (0.0s - 0.5s) with a pitch contour that suggests savoring or enjoyment. While there isn't a sharp, percussive 'smack' or 'click' sound typically associated with a literal 'smack of the lips', the vocalization 'Hmm' effectively conveys the sensation of tasting something good, aligning well with the semantic context of the sentence. The prosody is natural and fits the scenario of enjoying food.
reasoning_model_2: The model reads the text but struggles with the interpretation of 'Mm'. The vocalization produced at the beginning (0.0s - 0.7s) sounds more like 'Umm' or 'Erm', which is typically associated with hesitation or thinking, rather than the enjoyment of food. This mismatch makes the sentence sound less natural and disconnected from the intent of 'tasting really good'. Furthermore, there is no audible lip smack or tasting sound effect. The expression is somewhat flat compared to the enthusiastic nature of the text.
model_comparison: Model 1 is the clear winner. Although neither model produced a distinct, high-fidelity percussive 'lip smack' sound effect (like a wet click), Model 1 correctly interpreted the textual vocalization 'Mm' as a sound of enjoyment ('Hmm'), matching the context of the sentence perfectly. Model 2, on the other hand, pronounced 'Mm' as 'Um', which implies hesitation and contradicts the positive sentiment of the text. Model 1's delivery was naturally prosodic and appropriate for the scenario, whereas Model 2 sounded confused or hesitant.
